species: Hurricane-Force Ridge Wind -- sustained ultra-intensity howl, Cascade alpine ridge, rare storm
scientific_name: Meteorological event: orographically-amplified extratropical cyclone winds at Cascade Crest ridge exposure. No taxonomic designation. Beaufort Scale 12 (74+ mph sustained). Aeolian acoustics: von Karman vortex street tones from ridge obstacles.
sound_type: Sustained ultra-intensity howl. Broadband wind acoustic event, 20 Hz to 10,000+ Hz. Three simultaneous mechanisms: (1) Aeolian tones (von Karman vortex street) from ridge obstacles: f = 0.2V/D; at 74 mph, 1-cm obstacle = ~660 Hz; krummholz branches 0.5-5 cm = 200-1500 Hz; multiple obstacles = complex tonal overlay. (2) Turbulent boundary layer noise: broadband continuous, covers full acoustic spectrum. (3) Impact percussion: debris, ice crystals, loose rock in entrained flow. Total amplitude: 90-110 dB at surface, omnidirectional. E=9 through sustained maximum (hours) not peak gust (seconds).
ecosystem: Cascades ridge, Washington State high Cascades above treeline. 5,000-8,000+ ft elevation (1,524-2,438+ m). Exposed crest ridges with no forest buffer. Surface: rock (basalt, andesite, metamorphic), ice, snow, and krummholz (Subalpine Fir Abies lasiocarpa, Mountain Hemlock Tsuga mertensiana, Whitebark Pine Pinus albicaulis). Weather station records: Paradise/Mount Rainier 100+ mph gusts; Crystal Mountain 80-100+ mph during major Pacific storm passages.
season: Rare storm. Major wind events occur several times per year at Cascade ridge exposures. E=9 (sustained 74+ mph for hours) requires specific synoptic conditions: powerful Pacific extratropical cyclone + correct orographic orientation (wind direction aligned with Cascade cross-slope) + sustained pressure gradient. Not seasonal: can occur fall through spring. Most intense events: November-March (deep Pacific storms), but significant events possible into May.
family: Meteorology. Atmospheric dynamics: extratropical cyclone + orographic wind enhancement. Turbulent boundary layer acoustics. Aeolian tone physics (von Karman vortex street).
milestone: METAL SUMMIT SPS POSITION 3. Sustained ultra-intensity. The SPS entry most defined by duration of maximum intensity: not peak gust but hours-long sustained E=9 wind. Pairs with Soundgarden (S36-335): both defined by sustained maximum intensity, not by a single overwhelming peak. E=9 + E=9 = E=18. The howl that does not stop paired with the voice that does not break.

coordinates.description: Representative: Cascade Crest ridge exposures, central Washington Cascades. Crystal Mountain summit area: 47.0°N 121.5°W, 2000+ m. Norse Peak Wilderness ridge: 46.9°N 121.5°W. Chinook Pass crest: 46.9°N 121.5°W. Also: Washington Pass ridgeline 48.5°N 120.7°W; Mount Baker ridges 48.8°N 121.8°W.

acoustic_data.aeolian_tones: Von Karman vortex street: f = 0.2V/D. At sustained 74 mph (33 m/s): 1 cm diameter obstacle = 660 Hz; 5 cm diameter = 132 Hz; 0.5 cm diameter = 1320 Hz. Krummholz branch range 0.5-5 cm produces 132-1320 Hz Aeolian tone cluster. Multiple obstacles at different diameters and orientations produce complex tonal overlay against broadband background.
acoustic_data.turbulent_boundary_layer: Broadband continuous, 20 Hz to 10,000+ Hz at hurricane-force. Omnidirectional (listener inside acoustic field, not adjacent to point source). The primary energy contributor to the total acoustic level.
acoustic_data.impact_percussion: Entrained debris (rock, ice crystals, loose material) impact at irregular intervals and velocities. Impulse-type broadband transients. Secondary contributor to high-frequency range.
acoustic_data.total_amplitude: 90-110 dB at surface during sustained Beaufort 12. Speech communication impossible (ambient exceeds 90 dB). Dynamic wind pressure at 74 mph: approximately 130 Pa, equivalent to 15-20 kg horizontal force on standing person.
acoustic_data.e9_calibration: E=9 through sustained maximum intensity (hours) at exposed Cascade ridge. Distinguishes from E=8 entries: SPS-327 Mount Rainier above treeline E=8 represents the same environment at lower sustained intensity or shorter duration. E=9 requires the full orographic amplification mechanism sustaining hurricane force for extended duration.

nodes.title: Sustained Maximum -- Hours, Not Seconds
nodes.description: The key characteristic of the ridge wind E=9: not a peak gust but sustained hurricane-force wind for 6-12+ hours. Orographic amplification at the fixed crest geometry sustains the maximum when synoptic conditions are established. The acoustic event is continuous, not episodic.

nodes.title: Orographic Amplification -- The Venturi Effect at Cascade Crest
nodes.description: Pacific storm winds encounter the Cascade Range, forced upward by the terrain. At the crest ridge, terrain geometry narrows, accelerating flow (venturi effect). Valley winds of 50-60 mph become crest ridge winds of 80-110 mph. The ridge is the amplifier; the storm provides the input.

nodes.title: Krummholz as Aeolian Instruments -- Shaped by Wind to Play in Wind
nodes.description: Subalpine Fir, Mountain Hemlock, and Whitebark Pine at treeline grow in krummholz form -- wind-flagged, branch diameters 0.5-5 cm. These branch diameters produce Aeolian tones at 132-1320 Hz at hurricane-force wind. The trees are acoustically shaped by decades of wind into the instruments that produce the howl. The wind and the ridge ecosystem are one acoustic system.

nodes.title: Acoustic Totality -- Wind Masks Everything
nodes.description: In sustained hurricane-force ridge wind, all other sounds (bird, insect, water, vegetation) are acoustically masked. The wind occupies the entire spectrum and entire acoustic space. The listener is inside the event, not adjacent to it. E=9 includes this totality: nothing else is audible.

cross_references.relationship: E=9 + E=9 = E=18. Sustained vocal-guitar intensity (Cornell's four-octave range at full power through 79-minute Superunknown and decade of live performance) vs. sustained hurricane-force ridge howl (Aeolian tones at maximum, continuous for hours). Both defined by sustained maximum, not single peak. The voice and the wind, both holding.

Same Cascade alpine environment, lower sustained intensity = E=8. The ridge wind E=9 (335) vs. Rainier treeline wind E=8 (327): orographic amplification at Cascade Crest vs. standard alpine exposure. The crest amplifier produces the extra energy unit.

Both wind acoustic events in PNW. Douglas-fir crown (326) is canopy-level gale in old-growth forest: wind modified by trees, E=7. Hurricane ridge wind (335) is above-treeline exposed crest: wind unmodified, E=9. The forest transforms the wind; the ridge exposes it.
